text: The samples were analyzed and the results were obtained. But what caused them?
Two things move together, but one does not always cause the other.

There was a strong correlation between the dose and the outcome.
This does not prove causation: a hidden variable may confound it.
Part of the effect can be attributed to age, not only to the dose.
If the dose is higher, the outcome will improve.
If we control for age, we will know the truth.
If the variable confounds the data, the result will not be reliable.
If the effect passes the threshold, it will be significant.
Patricia, the data look great. You found a clear correlation between the dose and recovery, right?
Yes, a strong one. But I am careful: this is correlation, not causation. A hidden variable may confound it.
Good. Which variable worries you most?
Age. The higher-dose group was younger, so part of the outcome can be attributed to age.
So what is the plan? How will you prove a real effect?
If we control for age in the next trial, we will know whether the effect is significant above the threshold.
Correlation is not the same as causation.
A confounding variable may explain the result.

Good morning, everyone. Let me explain what the data show and, just as important, what they do not show. In our six-month study, we found a strong correlation between a higher intake of the new supplement and a better outcome in patients. When the dose was higher, the recovery score also went up. That is the link in the numbers. But I want to be very careful here, because correlation is not the same as causation. The fact that two things move together does not prove that one causes the other. There is a second variable we must consider: the patients who took the higher dose were also younger and more active. So part of the better outcome may be attributed to age and activity, not only to the supplement. This is what we call a confounding variable, and if we ignore it, we will reach the wrong conclusion. My recommendation is simple. If we run a second trial, we will control for age and activity from the start. And if the correlation stays strong after we control for those factors, then the result will be much more significant. Only then can we say, with confidence, that the supplement has a real effect. Until that point, we report a correlation, we name the threshold where the effect appears, and we are honest about what the data cannot prove.

Hi Patricia, this is David. I read the draft of your results section, and overall it is excellent. I have one important comment about cause and effect. In the third paragraph, you write that the supplement improves the outcome. Be careful with that wording, because so far we only have a correlation, not proof of causation. Two variables move together, but we have not ruled out the confounding factors yet. My suggestion is this. If you soften the language, the reviewers will trust the paper more. So instead of the supplement improves recovery, write the supplement is associated with better recovery. Also, please state the threshold clearly, the point where the effect becomes significant, because that number is what people will quote. One last thing. If we control for age in the next trial, we will know whether the effect is real. Let us add a short sentence about that plan. Everything else is solid, the data are strong, and the argument is clear. Call me when you have a moment and we will finalize it together. Thanks, Patricia, great work.

First, state the correlation you found in the data. Next, explain that correlation is not the same as causation. After that, point to a possible confounding variable. Then, say what you will control for in the next trial. Finally, name the threshold where the effect becomes significant.
